step.title: Create Instructions
step.goal: Write the command body - the actual instructions that execute when the command is invoked - into instructions.md beneath the frontmatter from step 4. Complexity depends on command type.

check.action:
Plan the instruction structure:
1. **Main instructions** - Core behavior description 2. **Argument handling** - How to process $ARGUMENTS 3. **Workflow** (optional) - Numbered steps if complex 4. **Rules** - Hard constraints on behavior

Keep it minimal:
1. **Invoke skill** - Single action to delegate 2. **Pass arguments** - How $ARGUMENTS flows to skill

substep.title: Handle Arguments
substep.action:
Define how $ARGUMENTS are processed:
$ARGUMENTS is OpenCode's runtime variable containing whatever the user typed after /command-name.
Common patterns: - Check if $ARGUMENTS looks like X, then do Y - If no $ARGUMENTS, ask or use defaults - Parse $ARGUMENTS for specific patterns
substep.ask:
How should this command handle arguments?
Examples: - "If $ARGUMENTS looks like a commit message, use it" - "If $ARGUMENTS specifies a file path, process that file" - "If no $ARGUMENTS, ask the user for input"

substep.title: Write Instructions
substep.action:
Write the main instruction block:
```xml <command name="{{command_name}}"> <instructions> Main behavior description.
<check if="$ARGUMENTS condition"> <action>Handle this case</action> </check>
<check if="no $ARGUMENTS"> <action>Default behavior</action> </check> </instructions> </command> ```

substep.title: Add Workflow
substep.action:
Add numbered workflow steps if the command has distinct phases:
```xml <workflow> <step n="1" title="Analyze"> <action>First action</action> </step>
<step n="2" title="Execute"> <action type="script">command to run</action> </step> </workflow> ```
substep.ask:
What workflow steps does this command need?
Keep it simple - commands typically have 2-4 steps max. If you need more, consider a skill instead.

substep.title: Define Rules
substep.action:
Add rules that constrain behavior:
```xml <rules> <rule>NEVER do X</rule> <rule>ALWAYS do Y</rule> <rule>Specific constraint</rule> </rules> ```
substep.ask:
What rules should govern this command's behavior?
Examples: - "NEVER add co-author information" - "ALWAYS confirm before destructive actions" - "Use conventional commit format"

substep.title: Write Wrapper
substep.action:
Write a minimal wrapper:
```xml <command name="{{command_name}}"> <instructions> <action>Use the {{target_skill}} skill to {{action_description}}</action> <action>Pass $ARGUMENTS as {{argument_usage}}</action> </instructions> </command> ```

substep.title: Save Instructions
substep.template-output:
Wrote instructions.md (body sits beneath the step-4 frontmatter):
```xml {{generated_instructions}} ```
[a] Edit instructions [c] Continue [y] YOLO

section.title: Instruction Block Structure
section.description: The <instructions> block is the heart of the command. It tells the agent what to do when the command is invoked.
section.template:
<command name="command-name">
<instructions> Main behavior description for {{user_context}}.
<check if="$ARGUMENTS condition"> <action>Handle specific case</action> </check> </instructions>
<workflow> <step n="1" title="...">...</step> </workflow>
<rules> <rule>Constraint</rule> </rules>
</command>

section.title: $ARGUMENTS Patterns
section.description: $ARGUMENTS contains user input after the command name. Common patterns for handling it:

patterns.pattern: <check if="$ARGUMENTS looks like a message"> <action>Use as the message or starting point</action> </check>
<check if="$ARGUMENTS specifies files or paths"> <action>Focus on those files</action> </check>
<check if="no $ARGUMENTS provided"> <action>Use default behavior or ask user</action> </check>

section.title: Workflow Steps
section.description: Optional numbered steps for commands with distinct phases. Keep simple - 2-4 steps max for commands.

rules.rule: Each step should have clear purpose
Use <ask> sparingly - commands should be quick

section.example: <workflow> <step n="1" title="Analyze"> <action type="script">git status</action> <action>Review changes</action> </step> <step n="2" title="Commit"> <action type="script">git commit -m "message"</action> </step> </workflow>

section.title: Rules Block
section.description: Hard constraints on command behavior. Use emphasis keywords for priority.
section.example: <rules> <rule>NEVER add co-author information</rule> <rule>ALWAYS confirm before force push</rule> <rule>Use conventional commit format</rule> </rules>

section.title: Wrapper Commands
section.description: Minimal structure that delegates to a skill.
section.template: <command name="pr-review"> <instructions> <action>Use the pr-review skill to address review feedback</action> <action>Pass $ARGUMENTS as the PR number context</action> </instructions> </command>

example.name: Standalone: /commit
example.config:
```xml <command name="commit"> <instructions> Create git commits following conventional commit style.
<check if="$ARGUMENTS looks like a commit message"> <action>Use it as the commit message</action> </check>
<check if="$ARGUMENTS specifies files"> <action>Focus on those files only</action> </check>
<check if="no $ARGUMENTS"> <action>Analyze all changes and organize appropriately</action> </check> </instructions>
<workflow> <step n="1" title="Analyze"> <action type="script">git status</action> <action type="script">git diff</action> </step> <step n="2" title="Plan"> <action>Group related changes</action> <ask>Create [N] commit(s)?</ask> </step> <step n="3" title="Execute"> <action type="script">git add [files]</action> <action type="script">git commit -m "[msg]"</action> </step> </workflow>
<rules> <rule>NEVER add co-author information</rule> <rule>NEVER use git add -A or git add .</rule> </rules> </command> ```

example.name: Wrapper: /pr-review
example.config: ```xml <command name="pr-review"> <instructions> <action>Use the pr-review skill to address PR review feedback</action> <action>Pass $ARGUMENTS as the PR number context</action> </instructions> </command> ```
